# Digestion and absorption

> *Follow one meal from plate to bloodstream. The first-year goal is **recognition** —
> trace food through the tube, name the organs, enzymes and hormones and their broad jobs,
> and understand how nutrients cross into the body. Not enzyme kinetics, not disease
> management — the map, not every street name.*

## From plate to bloodstream: the big picture

The gut is **one continuous tube**, mouth to anus. That has a surprising consequence:
whatever is in the tube is still, in a sense, **outside** the body — food only truly
"enters" you when it is **absorbed** across the gut wall. So the digestive system has just
two jobs: **break food into pieces small enough to absorb**, and then **absorb them**.

Doing this takes the **tube** itself (mouth → oesophagus → stomach → small intestine → large
intestine) plus four **accessory organs** that pour in juices along the way — the **salivary
glands**, the **liver**, the **gallbladder**, and the **pancreas**.

Rather than read the whole trip as a list, explore it: click each organ in the illustration
below to see what happens to your meal there.

<div data-island="gut" data-src="/diagrams/gut-journey.yml"></div>

## Moving the food: motility

Muscle in the gut wall moves food along with two patterns worth telling apart:
**peristalsis** — travelling waves that *push* the bolus forward — and **segmentation** —
back-and-forth squeezing that *mixes* the contents with digestive juices, mainly in the small
intestine. A useful line: *peristalsis pushes; segmentation shuffles.* Between the two sit
**sphincters** — muscular one-way gates (the lower oesophageal, pyloric, ileocaecal and anal
sphincters) that stop things going backwards.

**Gastric emptying is regulated, not passive.** A meal leaves the stomach over a few hours,
and — counter-intuitively — a **fatty or large meal empties more *slowly***, held back by
signals from the duodenum (this is partly why a greasy meal "sits heavy"). And between meals,
a cyclical "housekeeper" wave called the **migrating motor complex** sweeps leftovers and
bacteria onward roughly every 1–2 hours, triggered by the hormone **motilin** [@deloose2012mmc].

::: {.callout-note}
Transit times are **coarse ranges, not fixed numbers**: hours in the stomach, hours in the
small intestine, and up to a day or more in the colon — around **1–3 days** end to end,
varying with diet, fibre, fluid and activity. (How fibre speeds transit belongs to the gut
microbiome chapter.)
:::

## Chemical digestion: enzymes are scissors

Each digestive enzyme is a pair of **scissors** specific to one kind of nutrient, and it works
at a particular place along the tube [@statpearlsDigestion]:

- **Mouth.** Salivary **amylase** starts on starch (and quickly stops in the acid stomach); a
  little lingual lipase makes a token start on fat.
- **Stomach.** **Parietal cells** make **hydrochloric acid** (denaturing protein, killing
  microbes) and **intrinsic factor**; **chief cells** make **pepsin**, which cuts protein into
  peptides [@statpearlsStomach].
- **Duodenum.** The **pancreas** delivers **amylase**, **lipase** (with colipase) and
  protein-cutting enzymes, plus **bicarbonate** that neutralises stomach acid — the "antacid
  step" that gives the pancreatic enzymes the near-neutral pH they need.
- **Brush border.** The final snip happens right on the gut lining: **disaccharidases**
  (lactase, sucrase, maltase) split sugars, and **peptidases** finish the proteins.

| Site | Made by | Enzyme(s) | Breaks down |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mouth | salivary glands | salivary amylase | starch → smaller sugars |
| Stomach | chief cells / parietal cells | pepsin; HCl + intrinsic factor | protein → peptides |
| Duodenum | pancreas | amylase, lipase (+colipase), proteases + bicarbonate | starch, fat, protein → smaller units |
| Brush border | enterocyte surface | lactase/sucrase/maltase; peptidases | sugars & peptides → absorbable units |

::: {.callout-note}
## The enzyme safety-lock
The pancreas makes its protein-digesting enzymes in an **inactive form** and only switches
them on **once they reach the gut** — otherwise they would digest the pancreas itself. A neat
piece of biological safety engineering. *(First-years need the concept, not the named
activation cascade.)*
:::

### The everyday example: lactose intolerance

The brush-border enzyme **lactase** splits **lactose** (milk sugar). Most of the world's
adults make less lactase after childhood — and this is the norm, not a disease. In
**Singapore-born Chinese adults, about 95% are lactase-deficient**, yet only around a
**third** report symptoms on ordinary amounts of milk [@yap1989]. Three ideas that are easy to
confuse — and worth separating:

- **Lactase-deficient** — you make little lactase (very common in East-Asian adults).
- **Lactose-intolerant** — you get symptoms (bloating, wind) from a given amount; most people
  tolerate the lactose in **about a cup of milk** [@nihConsensus2010].
- **Milk-allergic** — an entirely different, immune reaction to milk *protein*.

When undigested lactose reaches the colon, resident bacteria ferment it and produce gas —
*that* mechanism belongs to the gut microbiome chapter. The practical message is food-first and
culturally aware: hard cheese, yoghurt, lactose-free milk and non-dairy calcium sources
(calcium-set tofu, fortified soy milk, leafy greens, small fish with bones) all work — "just
drink three glasses of milk" does not.

## The control panel: gut hormones

The gut is often called the body's **largest endocrine organ**: hormone-making cells are
scattered singly through its lining. Their shared logic is simple — food arriving in one
segment releases a hormone that **prepares the next** stage or **puts the brakes** on the
previous one [@statpearlsGIhormones]. Five are worth recognising, each as *where it's made →
what triggers it → its one job* (regulating **secretion and motility** — the role in appetite
comes later, in the chapter on appetite and weight regulation):

| Hormone | Made by | Trigger | Job |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gastrin** | stomach (G cells) | protein in the stomach | ↑ stomach acid |
| **Secretin** | duodenum (S cells) | acid arriving in the duodenum | ↑ **bicarbonate** (the antacid); ↓ acid |
| **CCK** | duodenum (I cells) | **fat and protein** in the duodenum | ↑ pancreatic **enzymes**; **squeezes the gallbladder**; slows emptying |
| **GIP** | duodenum (K cells) | glucose and fat | signals the pancreas that glucose is coming (an **incretin**) |
| **Motilin** | small intestine (M cells) | **fasting** between meals | drives the housekeeper sweep |

One idea to plant now: eating glucose raises insulin **more** than the same glucose given by
drip, because the gut warns the pancreas that food is coming — the **incretin** effect, carried
by GIP and a partner hormone (GLP-1) [@nauck2016incretin]. That everyday signal turns out to be
the basis of major modern diabetes and weight medicines — but their full story comes in later
chapters.

## Absorption: crossing the wall

### An enormous surface, folded away

The small intestine is huge **inside**, not outside. Its lining is thrown into folds, carpeted
with finger-like **villi**, and each cell tops itself with a fuzz of **microvilli** — stacking
up a surface area of roughly **30 m²**, about **half a badminton court**.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Not a tennis court
Textbooks long claimed the gut's surface rivals a **tennis court** (~200–300 m²). Careful
measurement puts it at about **30–40 m²** — big, but far less [@helander2014]. A tidy reminder
that even "textbook facts" get corrected.
:::

![Circular folds, villi and microvilli amplify the absorptive surface. Micrograph: OpenStax College, CC BY 3.0.](/figures/anatomy/small-intestine-histology.jpg){#fig-villi fig-alt="Histology of the small intestine showing circular folds, villi and the brush border of microvilli." width="420" .lightbox}

### Four ways in — and one star

Each absorptive cell (**enterocyte**) is a gatekeeper with two faces: the **brush border**
facing the gut contents and the far side facing blood and lymph. Nutrients cross by one of four
routes — **passive diffusion** (fats, water), **facilitated diffusion** (fructose, via GLUT5),
a **pump** using energy directly, and **secondary active transport**, the star of the show.
Here a **sodium pump** keeps sodium low inside the cell; that sodium gradient is a battery, and
**SGLT1** spends it to drag **glucose** in *alongside* sodium — sugar riding in on sodium's
coat-tails [@roder2014]. Absorbed sugars and amino acids (and small peptides) drain into the
**portal vein** and head first to the **liver**.

### Fat: bile, micelles and the lymphatic detour

Fat needs special handling because it will not dissolve in water. **Bile salts** — detergents
made by the liver — **emulsify** fat into tiny droplets so lipase can work; the products then
cluster with bile salts into **mixed micelles** that ferry them to the brush border. There the
fat **diffuses in** and the micelle stays behind (it's a shuttle, not cargo). Inside the cell
the fat is **re-esterified** and packaged with cholesterol and protein into a **chylomicron** —
too big for a blood capillary, so it slips into a **lacteal** (a lymph vessel) and reaches the
blood by the lymphatic back door, **bypassing the liver's first pass**. This is why blood drawn
after a fatty meal can look faintly milky.

![How dietary fat is absorbed: bile emulsifies it, micelles ferry it to the gut wall, and it leaves as chylomicrons in the lymph. Diagram: Health in Medicine, CC BY-SA 4.0.](/figures/anatomy/fat-absorption.svg){#fig-fat fig-alt="Schematic of fat absorption: a fat droplet is emulsified by bile salts, digested by lipase, ferried in micelles into the enterocyte, re-esterified and packaged into chylomicrons that enter the lacteal; short- and medium-chain fatty acids enter blood directly." width="640" .lightbox}

The reason bile works is written into its **shape**. Rotate the model below: a bile salt has a
**water-loving face and a fat-loving face** (it is *amphipathic*), so it can sit at the
oil–water boundary and break fat apart.

<div data-island="molecule" data-src="/structures/bile.mol.yml"></div>

*(The dominant story is that most dietary fat is **long-chain**, so it takes the lymph route.
The exception: **short- and medium-chain** fatty acids are small enough to enter the blood
directly, like sugars.)*

### Bile's thrifty recycling — and why one spot matters twice

Bile is **made by the liver**, **stored and concentrated in the gallbladder**, and released
into the duodenum when a fatty meal arrives (on the **CCK** signal). It is far too valuable to
throw away: about **95% of bile salts are reabsorbed** at the **terminal ileum** and shuttled
back to the liver to be used again — the **enterohepatic circulation** [@chiang2013]. The
terminal ileum is worth remembering because it does **two** jobs: it reclaims bile salts **and**
absorbs **vitamin B12**. Lose or damage it, and you can get **both** fat malabsorption **and**
B12 deficiency — "two casualties, one site."

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-enterohepatic
%%| fig-cap: "The enterohepatic circulation: bile salts are recycled, not spent."
flowchart LR
  L["Liver<br/>makes bile from cholesterol"] --> G["Gallbladder<br/>stores &amp; concentrates"]
  G -->|"CCK, after a fatty meal"| D["Duodenum<br/>bile emulsifies fat"]
  D --> I["Terminal ileum<br/>~95% of bile salts reabsorbed"]
  I -->|"portal vein"| L
  I -. "~5% lost in stool<br/>(replaced by new synthesis)" .-> S["Excreted"]
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Where each nutrient gets in (a link back to Chapter 4)
Absorption is often **site-specific**: **iron** in the **duodenum**, **vitamin B12** (with
intrinsic factor) at the **terminal ileum**, and the **fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)**
alongside fat and bile — so a fat or bile problem can quietly cause *their* deficiency too. The
nutrients themselves are covered in Chapter 4; here we're mapping *where* they get in.
:::

## Same food, different speed: how eating and preparation change digestion

Digestion depends not only on *what* is eaten but on *how*. Holding a food and its energy
content constant, its **physical form**, how it is **cooked**, the **order** in which the parts
of a meal are eaten, and what they are eaten **with** all change how quickly it is digested and
absorbed — and therefore how fast blood glucose rises, how much satiety is generated, and, for
some nutrients, how much is absorbed at all. The blood-glucose response itself (the glycaemic
index) is covered in the Macronutrients chapter; this section explains the digestive mechanisms
behind it.

| What changes | Mechanism (why the *speed* changes) | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Whole → blended → juiced | Cell walls and starch granules hide substrate from enzymes; disrupting them exposes surface area, and liquids empty faster | Faster, higher glucose rise; less satiety |
| Cooking, then cooling | Heat gelatinises starch (faster); cooling forms resistant starch (slower), retained on reheating | Cooked faster; cooled rice/pasta a little slower |
| Vegetables and protein before carbohydrate | The pre-load slows gastric emptying and stimulates CCK and incretin release | A lower, flatter glucose peak |
| Adding fat, protein, fibre or acid to a carbohydrate | All slow gastric emptying; acid also slows brush-border sugar-splitting | Blunts the glucose rise |

**Form and the food matrix.** In an intact plant food, starch and sugars are enclosed within
cell walls and packed into semi-crystalline granules that enzymes cannot reach until the
structure is broken. Juicing, blending and fine milling rupture this matrix and expose far more
surface to amylase, and liquids leave the stomach faster than solids — so digestion is quicker
and the glucose peak higher and earlier, with less satiety, even when the sugar content is
unchanged. In a classic study, apples taken as juice produced a larger insulin response and a
rebound fall in glucose, and were the least satiating, compared with the whole fruit
[@haber1977apple]. A whole orange versus a glass of its juice makes the same point.

**Cooking — and cooling.** Heat and water **gelatinise** starch, swelling the granules and
disrupting their crystalline structure so that enzymes digest them more readily; cooking also
denatures protein and improves its digestibility (the same principle by which gastric acid
unfolds protein for pepsin). Cooling cooked starch partly reverses this: some **retrogrades**
into *resistant starch*, which the small intestine cannot digest and which passes to the colon
(see the Gut Microbiome chapter). Chilling and reheating cooked rice or pasta therefore lowers
their glycaemic response modestly, and the resistant starch survives reheating [@sonia2015rice].
The effect is small and variable, and is not a licence for larger portions.

**Order and company.** With meal composition fixed, eating **vegetables and protein before the
carbohydrate** measurably lowers the post-meal glucose and insulin rise: in people with
prediabetes, a vegetable-and-protein-first order reduced the glucose peak by roughly 45% and the
incremental area under the curve by nearly 40% relative to carbohydrate first [@shukla2019order].
The mechanism belongs to this chapter — the pre-load slows gastric emptying of the carbohydrate
and stimulates the CCK and incretin responses described earlier. The evidence is consistent but
comes largely from small, short-term studies in prediabetes or type 2 diabetes, with weaker data
on long-term control; it is nonetheless a cost-free habit that suits a mixed-rice (*cai fng*)
meal — the vegetables and fish or tofu first, the rice last. Adding **fat, protein, viscous
fibre or acid** to a carbohydrate meal has a similar effect through the same brake on gastric
emptying; vinegar is the best-studied acid, modestly lowering post-meal glucose and insulin in a
meta-analysis [@shishehbor2017vinegar], probably by slowing emptying and inhibiting brush-border
disaccharidases. These are adjuncts, not treatments.

**Processing pre-digests food.** Industrial processing performs much of the gut's mechanical
work — grinding, refining and gelatinising — before food is eaten, so ultra-processed foods are
digested faster and more completely; their soft textures are also eaten more quickly and signal
satiety less effectively, whereas eating more slowly and chewing more modestly increases satiety
[@miquel2015chew].

::: {.callout-warning}
## What a controlled trial showed — and its limits
In a tightly controlled inpatient trial, 20 adults ate about **500 kcal/day more** and gained
weight on an ultra-processed diet than on an unprocessed one, although the diets were matched
for calories presented, macronutrients, sugar, fat, fibre and sodium; eating rate was faster on
the ultra-processed diet [@hall2019upf]. The trial is important but small (n = 20) and brief
(two weeks per arm) in a metabolic-ward setting, and the mechanism is still debated (energy
density, texture and eating rate are all candidates). Its *weight* consequences are taken up in
later chapters; here it shows how processing changes the **rate** of eating and digestion. The
point is not that all processing is harmful — cooking, fermentation and freezing are also
processing — but that minimally processed foods retain the structure that slows digestion.
:::

**Two mechanisms worth knowing.** Two everyday effects follow directly from earlier parts of
this chapter. First, **viscous fibre acts twice**: soluble fibres such as oat β-glucan and
psyllium thicken the intestinal contents, slowing both gastric emptying and glucose absorption,
and they **bind bile salts** so that more are lost in stool — to replace them the liver draws
cholesterol from the blood, which is why adequate viscous fibre modestly lowers LDL cholesterol
[@whitehead2014betaglucan]. This is the enterohepatic circulation described above, deliberately
interrupted (fibre *fermentation* is a separate process — see the Gut Microbiome chapter).
Second, **fat aids the absorption of fat-soluble vitamins**: vitamins A, D, E and K and the
carotenoids travel the same bile-and-micelle route as dietary fat, so a nearly fat-free meal
leaves much of them unabsorbed — carotenoid uptake from a salad was negligible with a fat-free
dressing and rose with added fat [@brown2004carotenoid]. Only a little fat is needed, a reason
to dress vegetables with some oil rather than eat them fat-free (the vitamins themselves are
covered in Chapter 4).

::: {.callout-warning}
## Food is not inert around medicines
Food can alter how a drug is absorbed by two broad mechanisms; at this stage the task is to
**recognise and refer**, not to manage.

- **Altered gut metabolism or transport.** **Grapefruit** irreversibly inhibits intestinal
  CYP3A4, the enzyme that normally breaks many drugs down before they are absorbed, and so
  *raises* their blood levels — a rare case of a healthy food causing a clinically important
  interaction, described for more than 85 medicines [@bailey2013grapefruit].
- **Binding (chelation).** Calcium and iron — in dairy, supplements or antacids — can bind
  certain drugs into an unabsorbable complex; calcium, for example, reduces levothyroxine
  absorption [@singh2000levothyroxine], with similar effects for some antibiotics and
  bisphosphonates. Separating them in time avoids the problem.

Alcohol is relevant here too: it injures the gastric lining and impairs absorption of several
nutrients, notably blocking the active transport of thiamine — part of the mechanism behind the
deficiency discussed in Chapter 4 [@subramanya2010alcohol]. The first-year priority is the
habit rather than the list: with any new medicine, check the label and ask a pharmacist about
food, grapefruit, dairy, iron and timing.
:::

## The payoff: a spoonful of sugar (and salt) saves lives

Put SGLT1 to work and you get one of the great public-health stories of the last century. In
severe diarrhoea (cholera, rotavirus), the gut pours out fluid — but **SGLT1 keeps working**.
Give a drink containing **both glucose and sodium**, and SGLT1 pulls the sodium in (with the
glucose as its handle); **water follows the sodium** by osmosis, and a dehydrated child
rehydrates **by mouth**, no drip needed. That is exactly what **oral rehydration salts (ORS)**
are: the modern WHO formula is a precise, low-strength mix (**75 mmol/L each of sodium and
glucose**) [@whoORS2006]. The underlying discovery was called, in a 1978 *Lancet* editorial,
"potentially the most important medical advance this century" [@ourworldindataORT] — and every
sachet of ORS in a Singapore pharmacy is that physiology in a packet.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Plain water is not the same
Plain water lacks the **glucose and sodium** that drive SGLT1 — the glucose here is a
*transport handle*, not a calorie source. That coupling is why ORS works and plain water,
alone, does not rehydrate as well.
:::

## Myths worth busting

Digestion attracts more folklore than almost any topic in nutrition — a good place to practise
evidence literacy:

- **"Gluten-free is healthier for everyone."** Not without coeliac disease or wheat allergy;
  going gluten-free can even cost you fibre and micronutrients — and crucially, if coeliac is
  suspected you must **test *before* removing gluten**, or the test misleads [@glutenFreeNonceliac].
- **"I need to detox / my 'leaky gut' is the problem."** Your liver and kidneys already
  detoxify; the gut barrier is real, but "leaky gut syndrome" as a diagnosable, supplement-fixable
  disease is **not established**, and there is no reliable test for it [@leakyGutMechanisms].
- **"Enzyme and probiotic supplements fix bloating for everyone."** Little evidence in healthy
  people; enzymes help only narrow cases (lactase, or Beano for beans), and probiotics are
  **strain- and situation-specific**, not a general tonic [@agaProbiotics2020].
- **"Spicy food causes ulcers."** Ulcers are caused by *Helicobacter pylori* and anti-inflammatory
  painkillers (NSAIDs), not chilli [@lanas2017pud].
- **"Fruit juice or a smoothie is as good as whole fruit."** Juicing removes the intact food
  matrix, speeding digestion and blunting satiety; across large cohorts, whole fruit is
  associated with *lower* type 2 diabetes risk and fruit juice with *higher* [@muraki2013fruit].
- **"Fat-free is the healthiest way to eat vegetables."** For the fat-soluble vitamins and
  carotenoids, a nearly fat-free meal leaves much unabsorbed; a little fat improves their uptake
  [@brown2004carotenoid].

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/digestion-absorption.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: "the drip-free rescue"

A worried parent brings in a toddler with two days of diarrhoea. Work through why this child can
be treated **by mouth** — and what makes oral rehydration actually work.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/ort-drip-free-rescue.case.yml"></div>
